Here is a comprehensive summary of the search results regarding **Shopify GraphQL Admin API Authentication, OAuth, Access Tokens, and Endpoints (2025)**:

---

## 🛍️ Shopify GraphQL Admin API – Authentication & OAuth (2025)

### 1. 🔐 Access Token Requirement

All GraphQL Admin API requests require a valid Shopify access token. You should include your token as an `X-Shopify-Access-Token` header on all GraphQL requests.

---

### 2. 🏗️ How Tokens Are Generated

Public and custom apps created in the Dev Dashboard generate tokens using OAuth, and custom apps made in the Shopify admin are authenticated in the Shopify admin.

A custom app can make authenticated requests to the GraphQL Admin API using the API access tokens that are generated when the app is installed on the store.

---

### 3. 🌐 GraphQL Endpoint

GraphQL queries are executed by sending `POST` HTTP requests to the endpoint:
`https://{store_name}.myshopify.com/admin/api/2026-01/graphql.json`

---

### 4. 🔑 OAuth 2.0 – Client Credentials Grant

The **client credentials grant** is an OAuth 2.0 flow where your app exchanges its own client ID and client secret directly with a store to obtain an access token, without any end-user interaction. This is ideal for trusted, server-to-server integrations owned by your organization.

Client credentials is only available for apps developed by your own organization and installed in stores that you own. Public or custom apps must use **token exchange** or **authorization code** flow.

After your app has obtained an API access token, it can make authenticated requests to the GraphQL Admin API. Access tokens are valid for **24 hours**, after which they must be refreshed.

---

### 5. 🔄 Online vs. Offline Access Tokens

When you create an API access token for the GraphQL Admin API, you can choose between two access modes: **offline** and **online**.

Tokens with **online access mode** are linked to an individual user on a store, where the access token's lifespan matches the lifespan of the user's web session. Online access is meant to be used when a user is interacting with your app through the web, or when an app must respect an individual user's permission level.

Tokens with online access mode expire either when the user logs out or after 24 hours.

---

### 6. 🔒 Access Scopes

To keep the platform secure, apps need to request specific access scopes during the install process. Only request as much data access as your app needs to work.

---

### 7. ⚠️ REST API Deprecation & GraphQL Migration

The REST Admin API is a legacy API as of October 1, 2024. Starting **April 1, 2025**, all new public apps must be built exclusively with the **GraphQL Admin API**.

---

### 8. 🛠️ Example cURL Request

Here is a basic example of an authenticated GraphQL Admin API call using cURL:
```bash
curl -X POST \
  https://{shop}.myshopify.com/admin/api/2026-01/graphql.json \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'X-Shopify-Access-Token: {SHOPIFY_ACCESS_TOKEN}' \
  -d '{ "query": "query { shop { name } }" }'
```


---

### 9. 📌 Dev Dashboard Token Tips

If you're sending your `client_id` or `client_secret` directly to the GraphQL Admin API, the solution is to first exchange your credentials for an `access_token` using the token endpoint, then use that token in your API requests.

Access tokens expire after 24 hours. The example code caches the token and automatically refreshes it before expiration — you don't need to manage this manually.

---

> **Summary:** In 2025, Shopify's GraphQL Admin API requires OAuth-based access tokens passed via the `X-Shopify-Access-Token` header. The primary endpoint is `https://{store}.myshopify.com/admin/api/{version}/graphql.json`. REST is deprecated, and all new public apps must use GraphQL as of April 2025.

Here is a comprehensive overview of the **Shopify MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server** landscape in 2025–2026, based on the latest search results:

---

## 🛒 Shopify & the Model Context Protocol (MCP): 2025–2026 Overview

### 📌 What is MCP?
MCP stands for Model Context Protocol, an open standard originally developed by Anthropic in late 2024 and now adopted by Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, and Shopify. It is an open standard that enables AI assistants to seamlessly and securely connect with your e-commerce platform — allowing an LLM (like ChatGPT, Perplexity, or a custom agent) to "ask" your store questions about products, availability, and customer preferences.

---

### 🚀 Shopify's Native MCP Support (Built-In & Free)

In mid-2025, Shopify rolled out built-in MCP support across all stores as part of the **Summer '25 Edition**. Every Shopify store now exposes an MCP endpoint (`/api/mcp`) by default, with no custom setup required.

Through this endpoint, an AI assistant can query and interact with the store's data in real time — for example, it can search the product catalog, retrieve product details (images, prices, descriptions), add or remove items from the cart, answer customer FAQs, and even initiate the checkout process.

Shopify handles the MCP protocol layer automatically for all merchants. You do not need to write code, install a custom app, or hire a developer to make your store MCP-accessible.

---

### 🧰 Shopify Dev MCP Server (Official)

The **Shopify Dev MCP server** enables your AI assistant to search Shopify docs, explore API schemas, build Functions, and get up-to-date answers about Shopify APIs.

- The MCP server gives your AI assistant access to Shopify's development resources so it can provide accurate code, solutions, and guidance based on current APIs. It requires **Node.js 18 or higher** and an AI development tool that supports MCP, such as Cursor or Gemini CLI.
- The server runs locally in your development environment and **doesn't require authentication**.

---

### 🌐 Open-Source Community MCP Servers for Shopify

A popular **free, open-source** community option is available on GitHub:

The **`shopify-mcp-server`** by antoineschaller is a comprehensive MCP server for Shopify Admin API integration. It provides **22 tools** to interact with your Shopify store, enabling AI assistants like Claude to manage products, orders, customers, inventory, analytics, and more through natural language.

Key capabilities include:
**Products Management** (list, search, create, update, and manage products and variants), **Orders Management** (view and update orders, fulfill orders, manage transactions), **Customer Management** (search and manage customer data), and **Inventory Management** (track and update inventory levels).

---

### 🎨 MCP UI — Open-Source Interactive Extension

A major initiative is **MCP-UI**, an open-source extension of MCP that allows AI agents to return rich, interactive UI components — not just text. Shopify's engineering team recognized that a text-only chat interface is limiting for shopping experiences, and in August 2025, published "MCP UI" — a protocol that lets an MCP server include embeddable UI modules in its responses.

It solves the critical challenge that commerce experiences require visual and interactive elements like product selectors, image galleries, and cart flows. This open-source protocol allows agents to embed commerce components while maintaining control through an intent-based messaging system.

---

### 📈 Ecosystem Growth & Adoption (2026)

As of early 2026, adoption numbers include **97+ million monthly SDK downloads**, over **10,000 public MCP servers**, and native support from OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify. In December 2025, Anthropic donated MCP to the **Agentic AI Foundation** under the Linux Foundation — making it no longer experimental technology, but the standard for AI-to-application connectivity.

In the e-commerce landscape of 2026, the primary "user" of your storefront is no longer a human browsing a screen — it is an AI agent. This shift toward **agentic commerce** requires a fundamentally different way of exposing data.

---

### 🔗 Summary Table

| Resource | Type | Cost | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shopify Native `/api/mcp` | Official | Free (built-in) | All stores, no setup needed |
| Shopify Dev MCP Server | Official | Free | Docs, API schemas, code generation |
| `antoineschaller/shopify-mcp-server` | Open Source | Free | 22 tools, Admin API integration |
| MCP UI Protocol | Open Source | Free | Interactive UI components over MCP |

---

In short, **Shopify's MCP ecosystem is free, open, and rapidly expanding** — with both official Shopify-built tools and a thriving community of open-source MCP servers enabling AI agents to power the next generation of e-commerce.

Here is a comprehensive summary of the latest information on **Shopify Admin API analytics, reports, orders, and sales data** for 2025–2026:

---

## 📊 Shopify Admin API: Analytics, Reports & Sales Data (2025–2026)

### 🔹 Overview of Shopify Analytics
Shopify reporting gives you visibility into every part of your e-commerce business, from sales and inventory to customer behavior and marketing performance. You can view analytics on your products, orders, visitors, and payments to gain insights into your business.

---

### 🔹 Accessing Reports in the Admin
You can find all your Shopify reports within your admin dashboard by navigating to **Analytics > Reports**. Staff members need specific "Analytics" permissions to view these reports and dashboards, which keeps access to sensitive data under control.

---

### 🔹 Sales Reports
Sales reports track the value of products and payments moving between you and your customers as you make sales and process reversals. The reports include every line item and shipping charge (if applicable) in an order as a separate sale item.

- Pending, unpaid, and canceled orders are included in the reports. Test orders aren't included.
- If you delete an order, customer, product, or variant, then its data will be removed from the reports.

#### 📌 2025–2026 Update: Return Metrics Changes
The existing return metrics and new reversal metrics will both display in your sales reports until **May 1, 2026**. After this date, the deprecated return metrics will be removed. Some names of certain metrics related to returns in analytics have been updated to *Sales reversals* and *Reversed quantity*. Additionally, the return reason for each line item is now tracked. This change distinguishes physical returns from order changes where items are removed without being returned.

---

### 🔹 Finance Reports
Shopify refreshes your Finance data every minute. Shopify's updated Finance Summary report gives a clearer snapshot of your store's financial performance, covering sales, payments, gift cards, tips, and gross profit for a chosen time frame. Key updates include detailed breakdowns of total sales and gross profit, payment methods and gateways, gift card activity, and tips by staff member.

---

### 🔹 Key Metrics Available
Key metrics include: **Total sales, Average Order Value (AOV), conversion rate, Customer Lifetime Value (LTV), and Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)**. Plan differences apply: Basic plans offer limited reports; Advanced and Plus open up custom reports, profit tracking, and cohort analysis. Most data refreshes within one minute. Export options include CSV format for analysis in Excel or Google Sheets.

---

### 🔹 Winter 2025 Editions: Dashboard Upgrades
With the Shopify Editions Winter 2025 updates, the dashboard became significantly more flexible. Merchants can now customize analytics using the metrics and dimensions they choose, with more personalized reporting rolling out across all plans.

The update introduced fully personalized metric cards that merchants can add, resize, reorder, and remove. Dashboard metrics now refresh continuously, eliminating the 12–24 hour data delays that plagued earlier versions. New bundle analytics track how product bundles perform relative to individual SKUs.

---

### 🔹 ShopifyQL for Advanced Querying
ShopifyQL now allows direct, real-time querying from within any report — a meaningful upgrade for data-fluent operators. Available exclusively on Shopify Plus ($2,300+/mo), ShopifyQL Notebooks enable SQL-like querying directly against your Shopify data.

---

### 🔹 GraphQL Admin API (2025–2026 Shift)
The REST Admin API became a legacy API on **October 1, 2024**. Starting **April 1, 2025**, all new public apps must be built exclusively with the **GraphQL Admin API**. Existing apps retain temporary access to REST, but Shopify will announce a full migration deadline with ample time provided for transition.

Starting **January 1, 2026**, you can no longer create new legacy custom apps directly from the Shopify Admin. New apps must be created using the Shopify Dev Dashboard at dev.shopify.com or the Shopify CLI. Existing custom apps are unaffected and continue to function normally.

---

### 🔹 Plan-Based Reporting Access
Basic Shopify plans provide a good starting point, but they can create blind spots for fast-growing brands. To access more advanced features like custom reports, cohort analyses, or profit reports that automatically include COGS, upgrading to the Shopify, Advanced Shopify, or Shopify Plus plans becomes necessary. The Advanced and Shopify Plus plans offer the most complete set of analytics, with richer datasets for detailed analysis.

---

### 🔹 Bundle Analytics
The *Bundle total sales over time* report displays the number of orders and total sales made with product bundles over time, with a default line graph comparing the previous 30-day period. The *Total sales by bundle* report displays the net number of items sold and total sales by product bundle.

---

**In summary**, Shopify's analytics and Admin API ecosystem is undergoing significant evolution in 2025–2026, with a major pivot to GraphQL, enhanced real-time dashboards, ShopifyQL query access, and updated return/reversal metrics. Merchants and developers should plan for the REST API deprecation and the May 2026 removal of legacy return metrics.

Here is a comprehensive summary of what the web search found about **Shopify ShopifyQL**, including querying analytics, page views, traffic, conversion rates, and API access:

---

## 🛒 What is ShopifyQL?

ShopifyQL is Shopify's query language built for commerce. Its reference documents the ShopifyQL syntax that you can use to query merchant store data and build analytics and reporting tools.

To make it easier for merchants to inform their decisions with data, Shopify built an accessible, commerce-focused querying language called ShopifyQL. It enables Shopify Plus merchants to explore their data with features like easy-to-learn syntax, one-step data visualization, built-in period comparisons, and commerce-specific date functions.

---

## 📊 Querying Page Views, Traffic & Conversion Rate

A practical ShopifyQL query for page views, traffic, and conversion rate by landing page looks like this:

To export the conversion rate by page, you can use the ShopifyQL query:
```
FROM sessions
SHOW online_store_visitors, sessions, sessions_with_cart_additions,
     sessions_that_reached_checkout, sessions_that_completed_checkout,
     pageviews, conversion_rate
WHERE landing_page_path IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY landing_page_type, landing_page_path
SINCE startOfDay(-30d) UNTIL today
ORDER BY sessions DESC
```


With the products model, users can understand how customers are engaging with product pages and progressing through checkout. Understanding the customer journey and identifying bottlenecks in converting visitors into paying customers is essential to improving revenue.

---

## ⚙️ ShopifyQL Query Syntax

Your query must include `FROM` and `SHOW` keywords with parameters. All other keywords are optional but must follow a specific sequence; ShopifyQL uses two-level indentation where `FROM` and `VISUALIZE` are top-level keywords, and all other keywords are indented under `FROM`.

Eligible metrics that can be ordered by time include `net_sales`, `gross_sales`, `orders`, `units_sold`, `customers`, `sessions`, and `revenue`. Ineligible metrics — those that can't be ordered by time — include `average_order_value`, `conversion_rate`, `cart_abandonment_rate`, and `growth_rate`.

The `COMPARE TO` keyword allows ShopifyQL users to effortlessly perform period-over-period analysis. For instance, you can compare this week's sales data to last week — making period-over-period exploration simpler and faster without needing to learn joins or window functions.

---

## 🔌 API Access

You can send ShopifyQL queries via the **GraphQL Admin API**, allowing you to build reporting directly into your apps or programmatically extract data for further analysis.

Shopify has built GraphQL endpoints for access to ShopifyQL and the underlying datasets. Developers can leverage these APIs to submit ShopifyQL queries and return the resulting data in the API response, saving time and resources by querying modeled data.

The ShopifyQL API uses **complexity-based rate limiting** rather than fixed queries-per-second.

---

## 📓 ShopifyQL Notebooks

ShopifyQL Notebooks is a powerful tool for querying data directly within your Shopify instance without requiring a Data Warehouse connection/service such as Snowflake or PowerBI.

A ShopifyQL query can provide insights into the performance of products, including their **add-to-cart rates**, **checkout rates**, **conversion rates**, **net sales**, and **average quantity purchased**.

In addition to merchants, Shopify partners can use ShopifyQL and the commerce data models to build apps that address niche merchant needs.

---

## 📝 Key Takeaways

- ShopifyQL is accessible through the **GraphQL Admin API**, Python scripts, code editors, or Shopify's analytics tools.
- You can query metrics like **page views**, **sessions**, **traffic**, and **conversion rates** from the `sessions` dataset.
- The `COMPARE TO` keyword enables easy **period-over-period comparisons**.
- API access uses the **`shopifyqlQuery`** GraphQL endpoint with complexity-based rate limits.
- ShopifyQL Notebooks is a free, in-platform tool great for quick analytics without needing an external data warehouse.

Here is a comprehensive overview of **Shopify GraphQL API for Reports, Analytics, Orders, Conversion Funnels, and Traffic Sources in 2025**, based on the latest available information:

---

## 🛒 Shopify GraphQL API: Analytics & Reporting in 2025

### 1. 📌 GraphQL is Now Mandatory for New Apps
Shopify has made GraphQL the definitive API. The REST Admin API has been marked as legacy, and as of **April 1, 2025**, all new apps submitted to the Shopify App Store are required to use GraphQL. GraphQL minimizes over-fetching by allowing you to specify exactly what data you need in a single query, improving performance by reducing the number of requests and the amount of data transferred.

---

### 2. 📊 Orders Query for Reports & Analytics
The `orders` GraphQL query returns a list of orders placed in the store, including data such as order status, customer, and line item details. It can be used to **build reports, analyze sales performance**, or automate fulfillment workflows.

The `orders` query supports **pagination**, **sorting**, and **filtering**.

- You can **filter by checkout token** to analyze conversion funnels or resolve payment issues — the checkout token references the checkout associated with an order.
- Only the last **60 days' worth of orders** are accessible from the `Order` object by default. To access older records, you need to request access to all orders.

---

### 3. 🔍 ShopifyQL: Commerce-Specific Query Language via GraphQL
As of **October 2025**, Shopify has enabled **ShopifyQL queries over GraphQL**, meaning developers can submit ShopifyQL queries and get data back exactly as it appears in Shopify Analytics.

You can use ShopifyQL queries through the **GraphQL Admin API**, with Python scripts, directly in code editors, or through Shopify's analytics tools.

ShopifyQL enables Shopify Plus merchants to explore their data with features like easy-to-learn syntax, one-step data visualization, built-in period comparisons, and commerce-specific date functions.

The `COMPARE TO` keyword allows ShopifyQL users to effortlessly perform **period-over-period analysis** — for instance, comparing this week's sales data to last week.

**Planned Visualizations:**
New visualizations on the roadmap include **dual axis charts, funnels, annotations, scatter plots, and donut charts**.

---

### 4. 📉 Conversion Funnels & Traffic Sources: Key Limitations

**Session data**, which is needed to replicate Shopify Analytics reports like conversion rates and funnels, is **not available through the GraphQL API**. Instead, developers should use the **Web Pixel API**, which operates differently.

Using the Web Pixel API, you can subscribe to Shopify's web analytics events and receive a webhook every time a user creates a session or views a page. Aggregating these events is straightforward (e.g., summing page view events) to match Shopify Analytics reports — however, you **cannot access historical data**; you'll only receive data from when your app is installed.

For **traffic source attribution**, the `Order` object includes data on a customer's visits and interactions with the online store before placing the order. This can be used to understand **customer behavior, attribution sources, and marketing effectiveness** to optimize your sales funnel.

---

### 5. ⚠️ ShopifyQL Rate Limits & Historical Data Constraints
Rate limits apply: submitting ShopifyQL requests over GraphQL is subject to the usual GraphQL rate limits, and there is no way to submit bulk jobs for ShopifyQL requests. If you need to send many requests in a short period, rate limits may cause an issue.

Historical data is also limited: hourly sales reports are only accessible for the **last 7 days**, some historical data like inventory levels is not available beyond **90 days**, and session-related metrics are unavailable before **October 1, 2022**.

---

### 6. 🛠️ Best Practices for Custom Reporting
For custom reporting, leverage GraphQL queries that permit **granular field selection**, joining key datasets such as `DiscountRedemption` and `CheckoutActivity`. Use incremental batch jobs for large data exports, paginating through records with cursor-based navigation to avoid API rate limits.

Prioritize key metrics: modern stores average a **1.8–3.5% conversion rate**. Monitor segmented conversion rates by **traffic source, device, and campaign** for granular optimization.

Use middleware analytics solutions like **Segment, Amplitude, or Mixpanel** to centralize data from multiple sources. These platforms collect user actions from your headless frontend and sync them with Shopify backend data for **full-funnel visibility**.

---

### 📝 Summary Table

| Feature | Available via GraphQL? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Orders Query | ✅ Yes | Supports filtering, sorting, pagination |
| Sales Reports (ShopifyQL) | ✅ Yes (Oct 2025+) | Rate limits apply |
| Conversion Funnel Data | ⚠️ Partial | Use checkout token filtering |
| Session/Traffic Source Data | ❌ Not directly | Use Web Pixel API instead |
| Historical Data | ⚠️ Limited | 60 days for orders; 7 days for hourly |

## Shopify GraphQL API — `shopifyqlQuery` Access Scopes Required

### 1. 🔑 Required Access Scope: `read_reports`

`shopifyqlQuery` requires the **`read_reports`** access scope.

The `read_reports` access scope is required to submit ShopifyQL queries over GraphQL.

---

### 2. 👤 Protected Customer Data (Level 2 Access)

In addition to `read_reports`, **Level 2 access to Customer data** — including name, address, phone, and email fields — is also required.

You will need approval (via your Shopify Partner 'App Distribution' page) to access any personally identifiable customer data, like emails, names, and addresses.

A common error developers encounter looks like this:

> "Access denied for `shopifyqlQuery` field. Required access: `read_reports` access scope. Also: Level 2 access to Customer data including name, address, phone, and email fields."

---

### 3. 🛠️ What `shopifyqlQuery` Does

`shopifyqlQuery` executes a ShopifyQL query to analyze store data and returns results in a tabular format. The response includes column metadata with names, data types, and display names, along with the actual data rows. If the query contains syntax errors, the response provides parse error messages instead of table data.

You can send ShopifyQL queries via the GraphQL Admin API, allowing you to build reporting directly into your apps or programmatically extract data for further analysis.

---

### 4. ⚡ Rate Limiting

The ShopifyQL API uses **complexity-based rate limiting** rather than fixed queries-per-second.

---

### 5. ⚠️ Known Issue: Works in Dev, Fails in Production

Some developers report that using `read_reports` works in development, but in production they encounter an "Access denied" error — typically because Level 2 protected customer data approval has not been granted for the production app.

---

### Summary Table

| Requirement | Details |
|---|---|
| Access Scope | `read_reports` |
| Customer Data | Level 2 PII access (name, address, phone, email) |
| Approval Process | Via Shopify Partner App Distribution page |
| Rate Limiting | Complexity-based (not fixed QPS) |

For more details, refer to the official Shopify docs: [shopifyqlQuery – GraphQL Admin](https://shopify.dev/docs/api/admin-graphql/latest/queries/shopifyqlQuery) and [Protected Customer Data Requirements](https://shopify.dev/docs/apps/launch/protected-customer-data).

Here is a comprehensive overview of **ShopifyQL**, its available datasets, and plan requirements based on the search results:

---

## 🛒 What is ShopifyQL?

ShopifyQL, or Shopify Query Language, is Shopify's query language built for commerce. Query languages are used to request and retrieve data from databases.

Unlike general-purpose SQL, ShopifyQL abstracts away database complexity and provides built-in support for common commerce patterns like time-series analysis, currency handling, and multi-store reporting.

---

## 📦 Available Datasets

ShopifyQL allows you to query store data across **sales, orders, products, customers, and sessions**.

Here's a breakdown of the key datasets:

### 1. **Sales Dataset**
The product exploration dataset allows users to explore all aspects of product sales such as conversion, returns, inventory, etc. The `sales` table is heavily used for financial metrics.

### 2. **Orders Dataset**
This dataset contains one order per row, and has data available starting August 1, 2018. Refunds, returns, and exchanges are captured, deleted orders are removed entirely. All gift card sale items are excluded from this dataset as well.

All metrics are fully additive, which means they are summable with any dimension in the dataset and are always the same.

### 3. **Products Dataset**
Alongside ShopifyQL, Shopify is building exploration data models that are able to answer questions across the entire spectrum of commerce: products, orders, and customers. Each model focuses on the necessary dimensions and metrics to enable data exploration associated with that domain.

### 4. **Sessions Dataset**
The main report types include Acquisition Reports that track how visitors find your store. These reports show sessions over time, sessions by referrer (source), and sessions by location, revealing which channels (organic search, social, email, etc.) drive the most traffic.

### 5. **Customers Dataset**
Users can calculate key metrics like total sales in multiple exploration datasets, whether the focus is on products, orders, or customers. This allows users to reconcile their work and gives them confidence in the queries they write.

---

## 🗂️ Multi-Dataset / Joining

ShopifyQL automatically joins multiple tables intelligently. The join field must have the same name in all joined schemas and must be in GROUP BY.

ShopifyQL supports querying from multiple data models and querying multiple tables in a single query with implicit joins.

---

## 📊 Key Features

- Analyze trends with built-in time-series support and date range comparisons, and build visualizations directly within queries for charts and tables.
- Eligible metrics for cumulative values include things that can be ordered by time, such as **net_sales, gross_sales, orders, units_sold, customers, sessions, revenue, taxes, shipping, discounts, or sales_reversals**.
- Available named date ranges include: `today`, `yesterday`, `this_week`, `last_week`, `this_month`, `last_month`, `this_quarter`, `last_quarter`, `this_year`, `last_year`, `this_weekend`, `last_weekend`, and `bfcm{year}`.
- Organizations with multiple stores can query across stores.

---

## 💳 Plan Requirements

ShopifyQL enables **Shopify Plus** merchants to explore their data with powerful features like easy-to-learn syntax, one-step data visualization, built-in period comparisons, and commerce-specific date functions.

ShopifyQL is limited to supported data models (e.g., sales, products, customers) and is **not available in all Shopify plans**; advanced analytics may require Shopify Plus development features.

Inventory reports are available on **higher plans**, offering snapshots of stock levels, like how many items remain of each variant at month's end or days of inventory remaining.

---

## 🔗 Access Methods

- Access ShopifyQL through the **code editor in the Shopify admin** with syntax highlighting, autocompletion, and real-time visualization.
- Build apps that query analytics data programmatically using the **`shopifyqlQuery` endpoint**.
- Access ShopifyQL data through a dedicated **Python SDK** with pandas integration designed for analytics workflows.

---

For the full dataset column definitions and types, you can visit the official Shopify developer docs at: [shopify.dev/docs/api/shopifyql/datasets](https://shopify.dev/docs/api/shopifyql/datasets).

Here is a comprehensive summary of what the web search results reveal about **Shopify ShopifyQL's `sessions` dataset** and its key metrics — `online_store_visitors`, `conversion_rate`, and `sessions_that_reached_checkout` — along with plan availability information:

---

## 📊 ShopifyQL `sessions` Dataset: Key Metrics

### What is ShopifyQL?

ShopifyQL, or Shopify Query Language, is Shopify's query language built for commerce. Query languages are used to request and retrieve data from databases. Unlike general-purpose SQL, ShopifyQL abstracts away database complexity and provides built-in support for common commerce patterns like time-series analysis, currency handling, and multi-store reporting.

---

### 📋 Example ShopifyQL Query Using the `sessions` Dataset

To export the conversion rate by page, you can use the following ShopifyQL query:

```
FROM sessions
SHOW online_store_visitors, sessions, sessions_with_cart_additions,
     sessions_that_reached_checkout, sessions_that_completed_checkout,
     pageviews, conversion_rate
WHERE landing_page_path IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY landing_page_type, landing_page_path
SINCE startOfDay(-30d) UNTIL today
ORDER BY sessions DESC
```


This query pulls together all the key session funnel metrics in one place.

---

### 🔢 Key Metrics Defined

- **`online_store_visitors`**: A metric accessible via ShopifyQL representing the number of unique online store visitors, alongside `sessions`, `utm_source`, `utm_medium`, and `referrer_source`.

- **`sessions_that_reached_checkout`**: The "reached checkout" rate refers to the number and percentage of sessions in which shoppers added one or more items to their cart, proceeded to the checkout, and performed an action (for example, a key press or mouse click). This number is usually smaller than the add-to-cart rate metric because some shoppers add items to their cart but don't go to the checkout or don't submit their contact information.

- **`conversion_rate`**: The conversion rate for each step is calculated as the number of sessions for a step divided by the number of total sessions. For example, the "Reached checkout" step is calculated as sessions that reached checkout divided by total sessions.

- **Note on `conversion_rate` in cumulative queries**: `conversion_rate` is considered an *ineligible* metric for cumulative time-ordered queries (along with `average_order_value`, `cart_abandonment_rate`, and `growth_rate`), as these metrics cannot meaningfully be summed over time.

---

### 📈 Related Reports in Shopify Admin

The **Conversion rate breakdown** report lets you visualize the path your visitors take through your store and checkout.

The **Conversion rate over time** report displays the percentage of online store visitors that make a purchase over a selected period of time.

**Sessions that completed checkout** measures the total number of sessions where a customer purchases a product. It is possible for a customer to make multiple purchases during a single session.

---

### 🏪 Plan Availability

**All merchants** have access to the ShopifyQL code editor directly in the Shopify admin. Navigate to **Analytics** and select **New exploration**, or open an existing report and click on the editor to expand it and edit the code.

However, the option to **display and filter reports and analytics for multiple stores** is available only to stores on the **Plus or Enterprise plans**. If your store is on a Plus or Enterprise plan and you have multiple stores within an organization, then you can modify your ShopifyQL query slightly to display data from multiple stores at one time.

On **any plan**, you have access to live data and summary metrics. For example, the customizable Analytics Dashboard displays key indicators (net sales, average order value, traffic by device, etc.) in easy-to-read cards.

Additionally, **Inventory Reports** are available only on higher plans, offering snapshots of stock levels like how many items remain of each variant at month's end or days of inventory remaining.

---

### 🛠️ Accessing Sessions Data via API

Metrics such as `sessions`, `online_store_visitors`, `utm_source`, `utm_medium`, and `referrer_source` are accessible in ShopifyQL reports, though automating this via the GraphQL API may have limitations. Some users currently have to manually download and send reports rather than using the API directly.

---

### Summary Table

| Metric | Dataset | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `online_store_visitors` | `sessions` | Count | Unique visitors to the online store |
| `sessions_that_reached_checkout` | `sessions` | Count | Sessions progressing to checkout |
| `sessions_that_completed_checkout` | `sessions` | Count | Sessions with completed purchase |
| `sessions_with_cart_additions` | `sessions` | Count | Sessions with cart adds |
| `conversion_rate` | `sessions` | Rate | Not eligible for cumulative queries |
| `pageviews` | `sessions` | Count | Total page views per session group |

> 📌 **Plan Note**: The core `sessions` dataset metrics (including `online_store_visitors`, `conversion_rate`, and `sessions_that_reached_checkout`) are accessible on **all Shopify plans** via the ShopifyQL editor. Multi-store cross-reporting requires **Shopify Plus or Enterprise**.

Here is a comprehensive, step-by-step guide on how to create a Shopify Custom App and generate an access token with `read_reports` and `read_analytics` scopes, based on the latest available information:

---

## 🛍️ How to Create a Shopify Custom App & Get an Access Token with `read_reports` / `read_analytics` Scopes (2025)

> ⚠️ **Important Note (2026 Update):** It appears that new custom apps can only be created from the Partner/Developer dashboard, and they now require an OAuth flow to get an access token, just like public apps. However, the steps below still apply for **admin-created custom apps** available to existing stores.

---

### ✅ Prerequisites

- If you have a staff or collaborator account on a user's store, make sure that the user gives you the relevant permissions to create a custom app and assign API scopes.
- In all cases, the account must also have the **Develop apps** permission.

---

### 🔧 Step-by-Step Instructions

#### **Step 1: Log Into Your Shopify Admin**

Log into your Shopify admin panel, go to **Settings**, then click on **Apps and sales channels**, and then click on **Develop apps**.

---

#### **Step 2: Create a New Custom App**

On the next page, hit **Create an app** and enter your **App name**.

---

#### **Step 3: Configure Admin API Scopes**

Click on **Configure Admin API scopes**.

In the scopes configuration area, search for and enable the following relevant scopes:
- ✅ `read_reports`
- ✅ `read_analytics`

> 📌 **Note on `read_reports`:** When trying to receive information from the Analytics API, you may be prompted to request access to `read_all_orders` and `read_reports`. Make sure to enable both if you're working with analytics data.

In the **Configuration** tab under the **Admin API integration** section, click **Edit**. Under the **Admin API access scopes** section, select the `read_<scope_name>` checkbox for the required scopes, then click **Save**.

---

#### **Step 4: Install the App**

Scroll up and click **Install app** at the right corner of the dashboard to get your Admin API token. Click **Install** to give this app access to your data.

---

#### **Step 5: Retrieve and Save Your Access Token**

Shopify will reveal the **Admin API access token**. Note that the Admin API token will only be revealed **once** due to data protection — copy and save your Admin API access token in a secure place.

---

#### **Step 6: Make Authenticated API Requests**

A custom app can make authenticated requests to the **GraphQL Admin API** using the API access tokens that are generated when the app is installed on the store.

The `AccessScope` resource allows you to retrieve the permissions that a merchant has granted to an app, such as `read_orders` and `write_products`.

These permissions allow apps to access data from a shop, and are granted when a merchant installs the app or updates an existing installation.

---

### 🔄 Rotating or Updating Scopes

- You **can't rotate API credentials** for custom apps created in the Shopify admin. You need to delete the app and create a new custom app which has new API credentials.
- If your app is already installed on a shop and you want the merchant to grant additional access scopes, you need to redirect the merchant to the app install page with the additional requested scopes.

---

### 🔒 Revoking Scopes (Best Practice)

If your app no longer requires certain access scopes from a merchant's store, it is recommended to revoke the access scopes to avoid a potential data leak if the access token is ever compromised. You can revoke access scopes by making a **GraphQL mutation**.

---

> 💡 **Also note:** The REST Admin API is a legacy API as of October 1, 2024. Starting April 1, 2025, all new public apps must be built exclusively with the **GraphQL Admin API**.

For the most up-to-date documentation, visit [Shopify Dev Docs](https://shopify.dev/docs/apps/build/authentication-authorization/access-tokens/generate-app-access-tokens-admin).

Here is the complete research report on Shopify's analytics API capabilities.

---

# Shopify Analytics API: Research Report

## 1. What APIs Does Shopify Provide for Reading Analytics/Reports Data?

### The Core Mechanism: ShopifyQL via the GraphQL Admin API

Shopify does not have a separate "Analytics API." Instead, analytics data is accessed through **two layered mechanisms**:

**Layer 1 — ShopifyQL (the recommended path)**

ShopifyQL is Shopify's commerce-specific query language, similar conceptually to SQL. As of October 2025, it is accessible programmatically via the `shopifyqlQuery` endpoint within the **GraphQL Admin API**. You submit a ShopifyQL query as a string, and receive tabular data back in JSON.

The available datasets are:

| Dataset | Key Metrics Available |
|---|---|
| `sessions` | `online_store_visitors`, `sessions`, `pageviews`, `sessions_with_cart_additions`, `sessions_that_reached_checkout`, `sessions_that_completed_checkout`, `conversion_rate`, `referrer_source`, `utm_source`, `utm_medium`, `landing_page_path`, `landing_page_type` |
| `orders` | `gross_sales`, `net_sales`, `orders`, `units_sold`, `taxes`, `shipping`, `discounts`, `sales_reversals`, `average_order_value`, `cart_abandonment_rate` |
| `products` | Product-level conversion, add-to-cart rate, checkout rate, net sales, average quantity purchased |
| `customers` | Customer lifetime value, total spend, order counts, acquisition cohort metrics |
| `sales` | Financial line-item level detail including returns/reversals |

A concrete example query for the conversion funnel by landing page:

```
FROM sessions
SHOW online_store_visitors, sessions, sessions_with_cart_additions,
     sessions_that_reached_checkout, sessions_that_completed_checkout,
     pageviews, conversion_rate
WHERE landing_page_path IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY landing_page_type, landing_page_path
SINCE startOfDay(-30d) UNTIL today
ORDER BY sessions DESC
```

This directly answers the page funnel analysis and traffic sources by page requirement.

**Layer 2 — Direct GraphQL queries on the `orders` object**

For raw order-level data, the `orders` GraphQL query returns individual order records with full detail: status, customer, line items, checkout token, source attribution. Supports filtering, sorting, and cursor-based pagination. Default access covers the last 60 days; requesting `read_all_orders` scope unlocks the full history.

**What is NOT available via API**

Real-time session tracking (Web Pixel events) cannot be retrieved historically through the API. The Web Pixel API only provides a forward-looking event stream from app installation date onward. You cannot backfill session-level raw events.

### Plan-Level Restrictions

| Feature | Plan Required |
|---|---|
| ShopifyQL editor in admin | All plans |
| `shopifyqlQuery` via GraphQL API | All plans (with `read_reports` scope) |
| ShopifyQL Notebooks (in-admin IDE) | Shopify Plus |
| Multi-store cross-organization queries | Shopify Plus or Enterprise |
| Custom reports builder in admin UI | Advanced or Plus |
| Inventory reports | Advanced or Plus |

---

## 2. Authentication Method

**For a custom integration (own store), the simplest path is a Custom App access token:**

1. In Shopify Admin, go to Settings > Apps and sales channels > Develop apps
2. Create an app, configure Admin API scopes
3. Enable: `read_reports`, `read_analytics`, `read_orders`, `read_all_orders` (for >60 days of history)
4. Install the app — copy the token immediately (shown only once)
5. All API requests use: `X-Shopify-Access-Token: {token}` header

**GraphQL endpoint format:**
```
POST https://{store-name}.myshopify.com/admin/api/2026-01/graphql.json
```

**For public apps (distributed to multiple stores):** OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow or client credentials grant. Access tokens expire after 24 hours and must be refreshed.

**Access scopes needed for analytics:**

| Scope | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `read_reports` | Required for `shopifyqlQuery` — all analytics datasets |
| `read_analytics` | General analytics access |
| `read_orders` / `read_all_orders` | Order-level data; `read_all_orders` unlocks >60 days |
| Level 2 Customer PII approval | Required if querying customer name/email/phone fields via ShopifyQL (needs Partner approval for public apps; not needed for private custom apps) |

---

## 3. Free MCP Servers for Shopify

Three options exist across a spectrum of "official" to community-built:

**Option A — Shopify Dev MCP Server (Official, Free)**
- Purpose: Helps AI assistants search Shopify documentation, API schemas, and generate code
- Does NOT connect to your store's live data; it is a developer tool
- No authentication required; runs locally
- Setup: Requires Node.js 18+, compatible with Cursor/Claude
- Docs: [shopify.dev/docs/apps/build/devmcp](https://shopify.dev/docs/apps/build/devmcp)

**Option B — antoineschaller/shopify-mcp-server (Community, Free, Open Source)**
- 22 tools covering products, orders, customers, inventory
- Includes `get_analytics` and `get_reports` tools
- Setup via `SHOPIFY_ACCESS_TOKEN` + `SHOPIFY_DOMAIN` environment variables
- 11 stars, relatively small community; depth of analytics tools not fully documented
- Repo: [github.com/antoineschaller/shopify-mcp-server](https://github.com/antoineschaller/shopify-mcp-server)

**Option C — GeLi2001/shopify-mcp (Community, Free, Open Source)**
- 31 tools covering products, orders, customers, draft orders, metafields, inventory
- Does NOT include dedicated analytics or ShopifyQL query tools
- Strong for operational/transactional operations
- Repo: [github.com/GeLi2001/shopify-mcp](https://github.com/GeLi2001/shopify-mcp)

**Option D — pashpashpash/shopify-mcp-server (Community, Free)**
- 15 tools, order and product focused
- No analytics tools
- Repo: [github.com/pashpashpash/shopify-mcp-server](https://github.com/pashpashpash/shopify-mcp-server)

**Assessment for analytics use case:** None of the community MCP servers expose ShopifyQL query capabilities directly. If you need funnel analysis and traffic data via MCP, you would likely need to either (a) use antoineschaller's server and verify what `get_analytics` actually calls, or (b) build a lightweight custom MCP server that wraps the `shopifyqlQuery` GraphQL endpoint.

---

## 4. GraphQL Admin API Endpoint and Getting Store Analytics

**Endpoint:**
```
POST https://{your-store}.myshopify.com/admin/api/2026-01/graphql.json
```

**Required header:**
```
X-Shopify-Access-Token: {your-access-token}
Content-Type: application/json
```

**How to call ShopifyQL from the GraphQL API:**

The GraphQL query wraps the ShopifyQL string inside a `shopifyqlQuery` call. The response returns column definitions and rows in a structured JSON table format. The API uses complexity-based rate limiting (not fixed requests-per-second); query cost scales with number of metrics, dimensions, and date range width.

**Key constraints to be aware of:**

| Constraint | Detail |
|---|---|
| Orders history (default) | Last 60 days only |
| Hourly granularity data | Last 7 days only |
| Session data history | Available from October 1, 2022 onward |
| Inventory snapshots | Last 90 days |
| Bulk export for ShopifyQL | Not supported; must paginate |
| Rate limiting | Complexity-based, not fixed QPS |

---

## Summary: Fit for Page Funnel, Traffic Sources, Conversion, Product Performance

| Your Requirement | Shopify API Coverage | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Page funnel analysis | Full — `sessions` dataset via ShopifyQL | `sessions_with_cart_additions`, `sessions_that_reached_checkout`, `sessions_that_completed_checkout` all available |
| Traffic sources by page | Full — `sessions` dataset | `referrer_source`, `utm_source`, `utm_medium`, `landing_page_path` available |
| Conversion rates | Full — `sessions` dataset | `conversion_rate` per landing page queryable |
| Product performance | Full — `products` dataset | Add-to-cart rate, checkout rate, net sales per product |
| Raw order data | Full — `orders` GraphQL query | Includes attribution, line items, checkout tokens |
| Real-time raw session events | Not available historically | Web Pixel API only; forward-looking from install date |

The ShopifyQL `sessions` dataset is the most powerful tool for funnel and traffic analysis. It is accessible on all Shopify plans via the API with the `read_reports` scope on a custom app token.

---

Sources:
- [Shopify GraphQL Admin API reference](https://shopify.dev/docs/api/admin-graphql/latest)
- [ShopifyQL with the GraphQL Admin API](https://shopify.dev/docs/apps/build/shopifyql/graphql-admin-api)
- [ShopifyQL syntax reference](https://shopify.dev/docs/api/shopifyql)
- [ShopifyQL datasets](https://shopify.dev/docs/api/shopifyql/datasets)

- [Generate access tokens for custom apps](https://shopify.dev/docs/apps/build/authentication-authorization/access-tokens/generate-app-access-tokens-admin)
- [Shopify API authentication](https://shopify.dev/docs/api/usage/authentication)
- [shopifyqlQuery GraphQL reference](https://shopify.dev/docs/api/admin-graphql/latest/queries/shopifyqlQuery)
- [Shopify Dev MCP server](https://shopify.dev/docs/apps/build/devmcp)
- [antoineschaller/shopify-mcp-server on GitHub](https://github.com/antoineschaller/shopify-mcp-server)
- [GeLi2001/shopify-mcp on GitHub](https://github.com/GeLi2001/shopify-mcp)
